We already did this book. We just finished it. As a matter of fact, we just finished it two days ago. So why in the world are we doing it again? Because that is the nature of knowledge. We have to continue to repeat until we learn. And that's really one of the big differences between knowledge and entertainment, right? You want to be entertained. Entertainment comes with novelty. It has to be new, fresh, something that you have not heard or seen before. It's got to catch you off guard. The mystique of it and the novelty of it is what makes you entertained. That's not what knowledge is for. Not at all. Knowledge warrants repetition. One of our scholars, he recently passed away. He said, fashion. He said, "A teacher who is not able to be repetitive, a teacher who's not able to teach the same thing over and over and over until the students absorb it and embibe it. A teacher who becomes wary of repetition is a failure at teaching. And a student who is wary of repetition is a failure as a student. That's what knowledge is. It has to be repeated until it's understood. Now, here's a really interesting question. Knowledge, what is knowledge? I mean, knowledge has a meaning in the Quran and Sunnet. Has a meaning in our terminology as people. When we speak we say this this is knowledge or this person has knowledge. What is knowledge? forman. Sai wasallam. Then on a sher. The candle in Knowledge is Knowledge is knowing Allah. Knowing the messenger of Allah, knowing the religion of Islam with evidence. That's what knowledge is. Anywhere in the Quran, in the Sunnah where you see the word knowledge used, it is only used to mean that knowledge of Allah, knowledge of the messenger of Allah, knowledge of the religion of Islam with the evidence to support it. That's part of knowledge. This is what knowledge is. So if we know that's what knowledge is, then the question then becomes who are the people of knowledge? The people of knowledge, they're mentioned in the Quran. And so the people of knowledge are the people who know Allah, know the messenger of Allah, know the religion of Islam with its evidences. That's what knowledge is. But here's an interesting point. Any information, any piece of knowledge that is not put into action is not considered knowledge for that person. Meaning a person is only considered of the people of knowledge in the Quran and Sunnah's terminology if he implements what he knows. If he puts what he knows into action, if he fails to implement and so knowledge is only that of it which is implemented. That's knowledge. And we could also say is knowledge just memorization let's say for instance someone memorizes the Quran are they a scholar of the Quran? Now not necessarily right is knowledge understanding without memorization. So let's say a person knows the the conclusions but they do not know the actual text. Is this person have knowledge? No. Knowledge is the marriage of understanding and memorization. That's what knowledge is. When we have something that is memorized and then it is understood, this is knowledge and implemented. >> Well, of course implemented, but that's in the terminology of the Quran, the Quran and Sunnah. This is why we have for instance, the scholars of evil, the people of the previous nations, they had scholars, but they were scholars of evil. Why? because they did not implement. One final thing, when we hear the word knowledge in the Quran, does it mean all and any kind of knowledge? summary: This lecture from the Muslim Community Center of Tucson discusses the Islamic definition of knowledge (ilm) as presented in the Quran and Sunnah. The speaker explains that true knowledge is not merely memorization or entertainment, but rather the combination of understanding, memorization, and practical implementation of knowledge about Allah, the Prophet Muhammad, and Islam with supporting evidence. The lecture emphasizes that repetition is essential for learning and contrasts Islamic knowledge with mere information or facts. The speaker also addresses how scholars are recognized, arguing that only other scholars can properly identify a true scholar, just as only pilots can recognize other pilots.
suggested_title: Understanding True Knowledge in Islam: Definition, Implementation, and Recognition of Scholars
catchy_title: What makes someone truly knowledgeable in Islam?
